skill.name: azure-cost
skill.description: Unified Azure cost management for querying historical spend, forecasting future costs, and finding optimization opportunities.
skill.summary: Query Azure costs, forecast spending, and identify cloud savings opportunities.
skill.author: Microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are false positives caused by markdown examples, Azure API field names, and relative documentation links. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. The remaining risk is moderate because the skill can guide authenticated Azure CLI, AKS, and Redis operations, including cost-changing updates and deletion recommendations that require explicit user approval.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Azure Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Azure CLI, PowerShell, kubectl, and azqr commands for cost analysis. These are instructional markdown examples, not executable skill code, but they can query tenant resources when a user is authenticated.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clearly present and require authenticated Azure access. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the files frame them as user-directed cost analysis workflows.

medium_findings.title: Destructive and Cost-Changing Recommendations Need Approval
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Redis deletion guidance and AKS tier update commands. Main safety requirements require approval, but these references can still lead to resource deletion or increased spend if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected files contain direct delete/update guidance, while SKILL.md adds an approval rule. This makes the issue a real workflow risk, but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Temporary File Creation and Cleanup Guidance
medium_findings.description: The workflow asks users to create temporary request files and later remove a temp directory. Paths are fixed local folders, but users should verify working directory context before cleanup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem actions are explicitly documented and scoped to temp/output paths. Risk is moderate-low because cleanup uses a fixed directory, but recursive deletion requires care.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings appear to match Azure Cost Management terms, markdown text, dates, or schema names rather than cryptographic algorithms used by executable code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic use for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed lines are markdown descriptions and API schema documentation. There is no executable crypto code in the cited context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Relative Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Path traversal findings are relative markdown links between local documentation files, such as links back to SKILL.md or nearby workflow references. They do not read arbitrary filesystem paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited paths are documentation links used for navigation. No code path consumes user-controlled filesystem input.

low_findings.title: Benign Azure Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft Azure documentation, Azure pricing pages, or Azure Status. No evidence found of third-party exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are visible Microsoft-owned documentation or status/pricing pages. They support user validation rather than hidden outbound traffic.

low_findings.title: Benign Environment-Aware Credential Examples
low_findings.description: Environment variable references are examples for selecting ManagedIdentityCredential versus DefaultAzureCredential. They do not print, transmit, or harvest secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use environment values to choose Azure identity behavior and explicitly warn against hardcoded credentials. No evidence found of credential exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Heuristic Dismissed: Code Execution Plus Network Plus Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic combined markdown command examples, Microsoft documentation links, and credential best-practice snippets. Context review found no executable code path that combines these into exfiltration or remote execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are real text patterns, but they occur in separate instructional markdown contexts. No malicious control flow or hidden execution mechanism was found.

content.value_statement: Azure teams need clear cost data before they can reduce spend. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code query costs, forecast budgets, and prepare optimization reports.
actual_capabilities: Build Cost Management Query API requests for subscriptions, resource groups, management groups, and billing scopes
Forecast future Azure spending with daily or monthly granularity when enough cost history exists
Break down costs by service, resource, location, resource group, and tags
Guide optimization reviews for orphaned resources, underused resources, Redis caches, and AKS clusters
Generate report structures with actual costs, validated pricing sources, and prioritized savings recommendations
Explain Azure Cost Management API errors, guardrails, pagination, and retry behavior
limitations: Requires Azure authentication and suitable RBAC roles before cost or resource queries can run
Does not deploy new resources or estimate costs for resources that do not exist yet
Forecast results depend on Azure Cost Management data quality and at least 28 days of cost history
Optimization commands can affect resources and must be reviewed before execution

use_cases.title: Monthly Cloud Cost Review
use_cases.description: Review current Azure spend, top services, resource groups, and cost trends for finance reporting.

use_cases.title: Platform Savings Review
use_cases.description: Find orphaned resources, underused services, missing tags, and rightsizing opportunities across subscriptions.

use_cases.title: Budget Forecast Planning
use_cases.description: Project end-of-month or future spend using Azure forecast data and recent actual costs.

prompt_templates.title: Show Current Spend
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Azure costs for this month by service for subscription <subscription-id>.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Cost Drivers
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the last 30 days of Azure costs by resource group and identify the top cost drivers.

prompt_templates.title: Forecast Budget Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Forecast Azure spend through the end of the month and compare it with a budget of <amount>.

prompt_templates.title: Run Optimization Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a cost optimization report for subscription <subscription-id>, including orphaned resources, utilization metrics, and approval-gated actions.

output_examples.input: Show this month of Azure spend by service.
output: Total month-to-date spend is shown with currency and billing scope.
Top services are ranked by cost with percentage of total spend.
The response notes missing permissions or unsupported dimensions when Azure rejects the query.

output_examples.input: Find cost savings in my production subscription.
output: The report lists actual cost drivers before recommending changes.
Each recommendation includes impact, evidence, estimated savings, and approval requirements.
Destructive or cost-changing actions are separated from read-only validation steps.
The forecast separates actual cost days from projected cost days.
It explains if Azure cannot forecast because the scope has insufficient history.
The summary highlights budget variance and major assumptions.

best_practices: Query actual Azure cost data before estimating savings or recommending changes.
Use least-privilege Azure roles and prefer managed identity guidance for production authentication.
Require explicit approval before running delete, update, or cost-changing commands.
anti_patterns: Treating forecast data as a guaranteed bill instead of a projection.
Running optimization commands before validating utilization, pricing, and business ownership.
Using broad subscription scans when the user has provided a narrower resource group or scope.

faq.question: What Azure permissions are required?
faq.answer: Cost Management Reader is required for cost queries. Monitoring Reader and Reader are usually needed for utilization and resource optimization checks.

faq.question: Can this skill change Azure resources?
faq.answer: The skill is primarily analytical, but some references include update or delete commands. Review and approve those actions before execution.

faq.question: Does it support AKS cost analysis?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes AKS cost add-on guidance, namespace cost visibility, anomaly review steps, and budget alert examples.

faq.question: Does it support Azure Redis optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Redis-specific review rules for failed caches, oversized development caches, premium tiers, and missing lifecycle tags.

faq.question: Why might a forecast fail?
faq.answer: Azure forecasts need enough historical data and a future end date. The skill explains common forecast API errors and fallbacks.

faq.question: Can it work across multiple subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes, if the user has access at a management group or billing scope. It can also guide subscription-by-subscription reviews.
